title: Bonded Structure Manufacturing and Discontinuity Types
narrationScript: Up to now most of our evaluation work has been welds and wrought products. This module is different. Bonded structures hold together with adhesive instead of fusion or fasteners, and the discontinuities that matter live in a layer thinner than a sheet of paper. To find them you first have to understand how the structure is built, because nearly every bondline defect is planted during manufacturing. Let's start with why engineers bond at all. A row of rivets carries load through a handful of small points, and every hole is a stress riser. An adhesive joint spreads the same load across the whole faying surface. That makes bonded assemblies light, smooth, and very resistant to fatigue, which is why aircraft skins, flight controls, doublers, and honeycomb panels lean on adhesive so heavily. The catch is that once the adhesive cures, nobody can see the joint, and no instrument can directly measure its strength. Ultrasonics can find gaps, voids, and separations, but a bond that is continuous and weak can pass sound almost like a good one. That is why process control during manufacture carries so much weight, and why you need to know the process. A structural bond starts with surface preparation. The metal is cleaned of oils, then chemically etched or anodized so the adhesive has a stable, active oxide to grip. On aluminum that usually means an acid etch or a phosphoric acid anodize followed by a corrosion inhibiting primer. Skip or contaminate this step and you get adhesion failure at the interface, the classic disbond. Next comes the adhesive itself, most often an epoxy film supplied on a roll with a carrier cloth that helps control bondline thickness, sometimes a paste for fitting and fillets. The details are stacked, metal skins, doublers, honeycomb core, and the assembly is vacuum bagged and cured under heat and pressure, typically in an autoclave at around two hundred fifty to three hundred fifty degrees Fahrenheit. Heat kicks off the cure. Pressure keeps the parts in intimate contact and squeezes out trapped air and volatiles. Lose pressure or heat in one zone and that zone ends up with voids or porosity. With composite parts you will hear three terms. Co-curing means the laminate and the bond cure in one shot. Co-bonding means a cured part is bonded to an uncured one. Secondary bonding means two fully cured parts are joined with adhesive. Each route changes where the weak interfaces sit. Now the constructions you will inspect. Metal-to-metal laminates are stacked sheets, a skin plus one or more doublers, each separated by a bondline. Honeycomb sandwich is two thin face sheets bonded to a lightweight core of aluminum or aramid paper cells. The strength of a sandwich panel depends on tiny adhesive fillets that climb the cell walls where core meets skin. Bonded repairs and patches follow the same logic on a smaller scale. Next, the discontinuities, and the words matter. A disbond is a separation at an interface, adhesive to metal or adhesive to core, where the layers should be joined. A void is a larger empty pocket inside the adhesive layer itself, usually trapped air or volatiles. Porosity is the same thing scattered fine. A weak bond, sometimes called a kissing bond, is the nasty one, the surfaces touch, sound crosses the joint, but the adhesion strength is low because of contamination or a bad cure. Ultrasonics generally cannot screen for it, which is why process records and witness coupons exist. Sandwich panels add their own family. Skin-to-core disbonds from impact or bad fit. Crushed or condensed core from handling or over pressure. Node disbonds where the cell walls themselves come unglued. Blown core when trapped moisture flashes to steam during a hot cure or service heating. And water ingress, where moisture sits in the cells, adds weight, freezes, and wrecks the core over time. Finally, location. Bondline defects cluster where the process is hardest to control. The edges of doublers, where peel stresses concentrate and pressure tapers off. Chamfers and ramps where thickness changes. Core splices where two blocks of honeycomb meet. The perimeter of repairs. Around fastener holes that penetrate bonded stacks. When you plan a scan, give those zones the slow, careful coverage. Walk through any bonded assembly in your head the way it was stacked in the shop, layer by layer, and ask where air could hide, where pressure was light, and where a dirty fingerprint would land. That mental map of the build is the foundation for the next lesson, where we put sound into these stacks and read what comes back.
imagePrompt: A composites technician in gloves lays a sheet of film adhesive onto an etched aluminum skin in a bright aerospace shop, with a honeycomb core panel and vacuum-bagging materials staged on the layup table beside her.

sections.heading: Why Structures Are Bonded
sections.body:
A riveted or bolted joint carries load through a few small points, and every fastener hole is a stress concentration. An adhesive joint spreads the same load across the entire faying surface. The result is a structure that is **lighter**, aerodynamically smooth, and far more **fatigue-resistant** than a fastened equivalent — which is why aircraft skins, doublers, flight-control surfaces, and honeycomb panels rely so heavily on adhesive bonding.

The trade-off matters to you as an inspector: once the adhesive cures, the joint is hidden, and **no NDT method directly measures bond strength**. Ultrasound finds geometric separations — voids, disbonds, porosity — because they interrupt sound transmission. A bond that is continuous but *weak* can transmit sound almost like a good one. That is why bonded-structure quality leans on two legs: tight **process control** during manufacture (verified with traveler records and destructively tested witness coupons), and **ultrasonic evaluation** for the geometric discontinuities the process can leave behind. To predict where those discontinuities form, you have to know the build process.

sections.heading: How a Structural Bond Is Made
sections.body:
A structural adhesive joint is built in disciplined steps, and each step has a failure mode:

- **Surface preparation.** Adherends are solvent-cleaned, then chemically activated — on aluminum, typically an acid etch or **phosphoric acid anodize** that grows a stable, porous oxide for the adhesive to grip, usually followed by a corrosion-inhibiting **primer**. Contamination here (oil, release agent, a fingerprint) produces adhesion failure at the interface.
- **Adhesive application.** Most structural bonds use **epoxy film adhesive** supplied on rolls with a woven carrier that helps control bondline thickness; **paste adhesives** fill gaps and form fillets. Out-of-date or poorly stored adhesive cures weak.
- **Layup and fit.** Skins, doublers, and core are stacked on tooling. Poor fit-up leaves a **starved** (too thin) or excessively thick bondline.
- **Cure.** The assembly is vacuum-bagged and cured under heat and pressure — commonly an autoclave cycle around 250–350 °F (120–175 °C). Heat drives the cure; **pressure maintains intimate contact** and squeezes out air and volatiles. Local loss of pressure or temperature produces voids and porosity in that zone.

With composite adherends you will hear **co-curing** (laminate and bond cure together), **co-bonding** (cured part bonded to an uncured one), and **secondary bonding** (two cured parts joined) — each route changes which interfaces are most at risk.

sections.caption: The two workhorse bonded constructions: a metal-to-metal laminate (skin plus bonded doubler) and a honeycomb sandwich panel with its adhesive fillets.

sections.heading: Built-Up Constructions You Will Inspect
sections.body:
Three construction families cover most bonded-structure work:

- **Metal-to-metal laminates.** Two or more sheets — a skin plus one or more **doublers** — each separated by a thin adhesive bondline. Common at reinforcements, splices, and repairs. Each bondline is a separate interface to interrogate, and they sit at different depths.
- **Honeycomb sandwich.** Two thin **face sheets** (aluminum or composite) bonded to a lightweight **core** of aluminum foil or aramid-paper cells. Bending stiffness comes from the core holding the faces apart; the joint depends on small adhesive **fillets** that wick up the cell walls where core meets skin. The far side of a sandwich panel is usually another bondline and face sheet, so panels are often evaluated from both sides or by through-transmission.
- **Bonded repairs.** Patches and doublers applied in service follow the same rules with less ideal process control — repair perimeters and field-cured bondlines deserve extra scrutiny.

In every case, think of the assembly as a **stack of interfaces**. Sound must cross each one, and each one is a place where the build can go wrong.

sections.heading: Discontinuity Types in the Bondline
sections.body:
Use these terms precisely — they drive different responses and different dispositions:

- **Disbond.** A separation at an interface where layers should be joined: adhesive-to-metal, adhesive-to-core, or adhesive-to-prior-laminate. May originate in manufacture (contamination, lost pressure) or service (impact, overload, moisture attack).
- **Void.** A discrete empty pocket *within* the adhesive layer — trapped air, bridged gaps, or volatiles that could not escape during cure.
- **Porosity.** The same gas entrapment scattered as fine pores; it weakens the joint and scatters sound without giving one clean reflector.
- **Weak bond / kissing bond.** Surfaces in intimate contact with little or no adhesion strength — contaminated surfaces or an under-cured adhesive. Sound largely crosses the contacting interface, so **ultrasonics generally cannot screen for it**; process control and witness coupons are the defense.
- **Bondline thickness variation.** Starved or overly thick adhesive from bad fit-up or excessive flow.

Sandwich panels add: **skin-to-core disbond**, **crushed or condensed core**, **node disbonds** (cell walls unglued from each other), **blown core** (trapped moisture flashing to steam during hot cure or service heating), and **water ingress** filling cells — a service problem that adds weight and destroys core as it freezes and corrodes.

sections.caption: Cross-section of a bonded doubler and sandwich panel marking where disbonds, voids, porosity, core damage, and water ingress typically occur.

sections.title: Disbond, void, weak bond — not interchangeable
sections.body: A **disbond** is a separation at an interface; a **void** is a gap inside the adhesive itself; a **weak (kissing) bond** is contact without strength. Ultrasound reliably finds the first two because they put an air gap in the sound path. It generally **cannot grade the third** — never report a bond as 'good' on strength, only as free of detectable separations to the technique's sensitivity.

sections.caption: Common bonded-structure discontinuities, where they form, and their usual cause.
rows: Surface contamination, lost cure pressure, service impact
Trapped air or volatiles, bridging over poor fit-up
Volatiles and moisture in adhesive, low cure pressure
Contamination or under-cure; not reliably detectable by UT
Handling damage, over-pressure, cell-wall adhesive failure
Core cells near edges, fasteners, damage
Sealing failure in service; steam expansion during heating

sections.heading: Where to Expect Trouble
sections.body:
Bondline discontinuities cluster where the process is hardest to control, so weight your scan plan accordingly:

- **Doubler and patch edges** — peel stresses concentrate there in service, and bagging pressure tapers off there in cure. Edge voids and edge disbonds are the classic finds.
- **Chamfers, ramps, and steps** — thickness transitions where film adhesive can bridge and trap air.
- **Core splices** — where two blocks of honeycomb meet with a foaming adhesive; gaps and unexpanded splice adhesive hide here.
- **Repair perimeters** — field-cured bondlines with portable heat and vacuum, the least controlled process you will evaluate.
- **Fastener penetrations** — sealing failures admit water into core; nearby bondlines see drilling loads.

Before you couple a probe to a bonded part, sketch its stack-up: every layer, every interface, every depth. Knowing what a *good* assembly looks like layer by layer is what lets you recognize a bad one on the screen — which is exactly where the next lesson picks up.

Typical bondline discontinuities and where they occur

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a riveted joint, the main structural advantage of an adhesive-bonded joint is that it:
options: Can be disassembled easily for inspection
Spreads load over the whole faying surface instead of concentrating it at fastener holes
Is stronger in peel than in shear
Requires no surface preparation before assembly
checkQuestions.explanation: Adhesive distributes load across the entire bonded area, eliminating the stress concentrations of fastener holes — the source of bonding's weight and fatigue advantages.

checkQuestions.question: During autoclave cure of a bonded assembly, the applied pressure primarily serves to:
options: Accelerate the chemical cure reaction
Harden the aluminum face sheets
Keep the adherends in intimate contact and squeeze out trapped air and volatiles
Etch the faying surfaces for better adhesion
checkQuestions.explanation: Heat drives the cure chemistry; pressure maintains contact and expels gas. A zone that loses pressure during cure typically ends up with voids and porosity.

checkQuestions.question: A separation between the adhesive layer and the aluminum skin it should grip is properly called a:
options: Void
Disbond
Porosity cluster
Core node failure
checkQuestions.explanation: A disbond is a separation at a bonded interface (adhesive-to-adherend or adhesive-to-core); a void is a gas pocket within the adhesive layer itself.

checkQuestions.question: Why is a kissing (weak) bond a special problem for ultrasonic evaluation?
options: It only occurs in honeycomb core, where sound cannot reach
It reflects all sound and saturates the screen
The surfaces remain in intimate contact, so sound largely crosses the joint and the weakness goes undetected
It changes the adhesive's velocity so much that calibration is impossible
checkQuestions.explanation: With the surfaces touching, there is no air gap to reflect sound, so transmission looks nearly normal. Weak-bond protection comes from process control and witness coupons, not UT.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of locations deserves extra scan attention because bondline defects concentrate there?
options: Doubler edges, core splices, ramps and chamfers, repair perimeters
The geometric center of every panel, far from edges
Only areas more than 25 mm from any fastener
Painted surfaces, because paint causes disbonds
checkQuestions.explanation: Peel stresses, tapering bag pressure, splice gaps, and field-cure limitations make doubler edges, splices, thickness transitions, and repair perimeters the classic defect sites.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §4.1 (bonded structures: manufacturing processes)
CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §4.2 (bonded structures: types of discontinuities)
